data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Time To Buy Before The Market Rebounds Further
data.description:
EPD: So Much Fear Yet Still Bottomed

Just when we thought energy stocks (including the high-quality energy infra plays) could flounder for the rest of 2025 (or potentially even through 2026… who knows?), the detente between the US and China sparked a risk-on rally that arguably took bearish investors by surprise.

The “V” bottom recovery has reclaimed most, if not all, of the losses for the Nasdaq (QQQ) (NDX) and the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) since the market topped out in February 2025. Even the energy sector (XLE) also marked a constructive revival in fortunes from the bottom in April, as investors lowered the threat of a global recession, while energy prices also staged their mini recovery of sorts.

As a result, we have moved past the pivotal $50 crude oil pricing zone that could see US production start to reverse its years of shale-driven gains that spurred America into the leading oil producer globally. With that, America’s leading oil & gas companies are also blessed with the byproducts that include natural gas and NGL, benefiting across the energy value chain.

For Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD), the respite couldn’t have been more keenly felt, as EPD also marked its April nadir, Dip buyers clearly saw value in EPD’s forward distribution yield of 7.6% at the recent lows, affording investors an amazing opportunity to snap up units of the leading energy infrastructure company. With a nearly 17% price recovery (not including distribution) from those lows, I assess that its 6.9% yield still looks attractively valued, even when considering that the 10Y (US10Y) still printed 4.45% as it closed the week.

While the rise in long-term yields could impose more pressure on the recovery of EPD, the resilience of the rally over the past month has assured me that high-quality midstream plays like Enterprise Products Partners are well-poised to ride the abatement of trade tensions that should boost energy flow again between the world’s top two economic superpowers, and also rest of the world.

In my previous EPD article, I reminded investors why the quality of the LP and its stable cash flows should provide much-needed sturdiness to income investors seeking a “safer” harbor to dodge the trade tensions. Yet, the fear-induced plunge spurred by Trump’s Liberation Day reminds us why there is nowhere to hide when the market goes on a selling frenzy, although high conviction investors probably viewed such market anxieties as “perfect” opportunities to snap up even more EPD units as fearful investors bailed out.

As a reminder, EPD management underscored its confidence that pricing dynamics would help to resolve the changes in production levels, urging investors to let pricing work its way through the market accordingly.

EPD: Balancing Growth And Capital Return

In addition, EPD hasn’t observed substantial revisions to its production outlook, as it maintained its $4B to $4.5B in CapEx for 2025, while pointing to a significantly reduced capital outlay of $2B to $2.5B next year. In other words, the climate remains conducive for EPD to continue pushing through its $7.6B in capital growth projects, of which $6B has been earmarked for 2025. With crude oil prices having taken back to the $60 level recently, it has dodged the critical $50 level that EPD management emphasized could lead to smaller independent producers pulling back from production, but possibly not the larger ones.

Furthermore, given EPD’s role across the energy value chain, its well-diversified (while concentrated in NGL access) strategy helps shield it from previous fears that China could also hit LPG with tariffs. However, management clarified that most of its capacity remains filled to the brim contractually, alleviating undue worries that could afflict even the top energy infra players through 2026.

In other words, unless we head into a global recession (which was never my base case, with a slowdown more likely), I have confidence that EPD’s solid financial profile, excess DCF reaching $3.6B next year, and ongoing capital allocation framework to continue driving shareholder value should mitigate selling intensity that we saw occur in April.

In addition, as the LP moves past its heightened capital spending priorities (front-loading of CapEx) in 2025, it should also offer a buffer for EPD to reallocate higher capital returns to unit holders from 2026, even if contracted capacity were to weaken post-2026. Yet, with 80% of its revenue imputed to fee-based income, I believe the investment thesis in EPD remains sound, since its valuation has also normalized through the recent lows.

Buy Or Sell EPD Units?

As seen above, EPD’s valuation has remained stable and supported whenever selling pressure attempted to hobble investors at the 9.1x forward EBITDA level. That zone is perched against the -1 standard deviation zone below its 10Y average, highlighting relative pessimism.

Yet, I don’t think a return to the heydays of above 11.2x EBITDA is reasonable in the current context, as investors navigate the long-term shift in oil and gas production in favor of green energy and lower carbon intensity prospects. Moreover, EPD’s lowered CapEx outlay from 2026 suggests that such a heightened spending environment isn’t anticipated to persist, with capital discipline and capital return as the main narrative underlining EPD’s long-term thesis.

Moreover, interest rates are expected to remain relatively high as the market digests the long-term structural changes to the US economy attributed to Trump’s fundamental restructuring. Hence, it should temper the outlook for lower rates through 2026, even as recent inflation reports have headed in the correct direction of more rate cuts over the next twelve months.

Yet, EPD’s valuation remains markedly below the highs it struck in 2025, suggesting that we should have an opportunity to enjoy a more sustained recovery through the rest of the year. However, we must be cognizant that Trump’s trade tariff pauses have a 90-day limit (including the recent hiatus with China), intimating that near-term uncertainties could still persist, lowering buying sentiments on EPD to reach levels struck earlier this year.

As seen above, EPD has climbed back up its 50-week moving average (blue line), a pivotal zone to ascertain the strength of its underlying buying sentiments. In other words, I’m increasingly certain that EPD’s uptrend bias (clearly seen) remains robust. Also, the sharp vertical drop in April hasn’t deterred investors from picking up the pieces with those highly appealing yields. Therefore, I assess that investors have remained confident in EPD’s prospects across the energy value chain through its leading role in energy infrastructure.

However, I must highlight that the $34.5 zone has seen strong selling pressure that resisted buying advances between November 2024 and March this year. Hence, investors should avoid entering too aggressively close to that zone, particularly as EPD has also reached overbought levels. For now, the bullish thesis looks set to continue, as momentum returns to energy infra plays.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BASF: Earnings Remains Tough, Neutral Rating Confirmed
data.description:
We have historically maintained a long-biased view on BASF (OTCQX:BASFY)(OTCQX:BFFAF), but last year we prudently shifted our rating to neutral — a move that proved timely (Fig. 1). Looking ahead, we continue to see merit in maintaining an equal-weight position over a twelve-month horizon. Even after an unusually long destocking period, we believe EU chemicals volumes are bottoming out, and see much more compelling cyclical equity stories such as Arkema. Given the recent BASF development (potential IPO of the Agriculture division and intention to sell its Coatings assets), we are back to comment on the equity story.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Neutral Take

BASF delivered a Q1 EBITDA of €2.62 billion (Fig. 2), which was down by 3% year-on-year. Turnover reached €17.40 billion and was also down by 1% on a comparable basis. This was due to lower price (-0.9%), a FX tailwind (0.4%), partially offset by volumes (-0.9%) and portfolio changes (+0.2%). The key to the report is the fact that net financial debt (excluding a pension deficit of €2.15 billion) stood at €20.39 billion (2.6x EBITDA). By operational MIX, upstream and downstream were down by 16% and 9%, respectively. By division, the company reported supportive results in Nutrition & Care and Chemicals, while lower results in Coatings, Battery Materials, and Agriculture Solutions. BASF recorded an outflow of almost €1.8 billion in free cash flow due to working capital requirements and lower earnings, which were also partially offset by lower CAPEX investment.

Source: BASF Q1 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still Neutral?

  1. Despite confirming full-year EBITDA guidance (Fig. 4), management acknowledged the current uncertainty, stating, "A reliable quantification of the impact on the global economy is not possible at this time. In light of the volatile situation, the assumptions published in the BASF Report 2024 regarding the global economic environment in 2025 remain unchanged for the time being. ” This highlights the limited visibility in the future. In number, the CEO has reiterated its Fiscal Year 2025 EBITDA range for €8-8.4 billion, with FCF estimates between €400-800 million. On the impact of tariffs, in the Q1 press release, the management commented, “Production momentum in the chemical industry and its customer industries was significantly influenced by reactions to anticipated tariff increases by the United States. Developments going forward will largely depend on the trade policy decisions made by the United States and its trading partners." We understand this stance, but this assumes a more downside risk scenario than notable improvement from current conditions. Therefore, we prefer to remain on the sidelines.

  2. Including pension, BASF's net debt is above €20 billion (Fig. 3), with a leverage ratio on the EBITDA above 2.5x. There is a viable roadmap that assumes disposal monetisation. This will likely reduce the debt by approximately €10 billion (monetisation of the coatings division and Agricultural assets); however, the disposal needs to be structured, and it is hard to anticipate the upfront proceeds for the company. That said, on a negative note, if BASF sells these assets, its cash flows and the residual portfolio MIX will have a more volatile profile.

  3. Considering its debt level and complex disposal evolution, we believe the company needs to undergo a turnaround to reduce costs. As such, we think BASF should restructure its European footprint. This will be carried with potential proceeds and will likely limit buyback capacity.

  4. With the ramp-up in China, the current chemicals spread far below the level embedded within the guide, which suggests that this project could contribute €1 billion of EBITDA. In the EU, there is a substantial oversupply environment in most commodities. Even if there were closures, the pricing and margin environment in most of the company’s commodity MIX segments will likely remain under pressure.

Fig 3

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Our last analysis reported higher leverage in the future, "at least €1 billion per year over the next three years," and was "not including BASF share buyback estimates." We continue to believe that. BASF’s large new greenfield site in China will start this year and should help earnings; however, the company highlighted that margins remain tough. EU industry overcapacity is a factor, and FX is also an incremental headwind. With an uncertainty management tone, we project BASF EBITDA at its low-end guidance of €8 billion (Fig. 4). In our previous valuation, we used a mid-cycle EBITDA projection to value the company. This is not the case, so with a €8 billion EBITDA, and the company's technical guidance on CAPEX (for D&A, we estimate almost €4 billion), interest, and a corporate tax rate of 25%, BASF adjusted net income reached €3 billion with an EPS of €3.8. This considers the current outstanding shares.

For the valuation, we applied a blended methodology with an EV/EBITDA of 8x and a P/E of 12x. Our multiple is 20% lower vs. the company's median multiple (Fig. 5) and reflects: 1) lower DPS, high earnings uncertainty, and 3) potential downside to Wall Street consensus estimates. Considering the net debt, we reached a target price of €47.2 ($13.2 in ADR). This is slightly lower than our previous target price of €49 per share.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Risks

We initiated BASF in 2022 and have long covered its downside risks. Thus, we report the following: 1) industry capacity evolution combined with supply/demand imbalances, 2) inventory cycles, and shocked from tariffs, 3) changes in commodity pricing with impact on oil and gas prices, 4) higher interest rates given BASF net debt evolution, and 5) FX changes. Additionally, we now report disposal proceeds and execution risks. BASF is also subject to lower economic activity, especially in Europe. Product litigation, CAPEX inflation, and potential environmental/safety liabilities are other risks to consider.

Conclusion

After the Q1 results, BASF offers higher downside risks with a heavy balance sheet compared to peers. We rate BASF neutral, with an expensive valuation relative to other cyclical chemical stocks within our coverage. In the long term, we like the company's ongoing portfolio ratio but expect structural performance improvements in Europe.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CEF Weekly Wrap: Amundi Gives Up On CEF Shareholders
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund ("CEF") market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the second week of May. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

Most CEF sectors saw higher NAVs as risk sentiment remained upbeat, however discounts were mixed. MLP and Convertible funds outperformed on the week. Month-to-date, equity linked sectors are in the lead, reflecting the continued rally in stocks.

The median CEF sector discount remains on the tight side historically.

Our credit CEF swirlogram has swerved back towards the expensive top-left valuation quadrant that features relatively tight credit spreads and discounts, making new allocations less compelling at current levels.

Market Themes

Pioneer CEFs will be liquidating. These include PHT, MAV, HNW, MHI, PHD, MIO - a combination of taxable and tax-exempt funds. Recall that Amundi acquired Pioneer in 2016 but kept the name for the funds. Last year Amundi US merged with Victory Capital. In connection with the transaction, fund shareholders were asked to approve a new investment advisory agreement in March 2025.

At the meeting, the company did not obtain a sufficient number of votes to approve the agreement and had to postpone the meeting. After some thought, it threw in the towel and decided to liquidate the funds instead of holding another vote.

This is a lose-lose proposition for both the manager and shareholders. The company loses management fees and shareholders are booted out of the fund they have been holding. It’s certainly possible to argue that shareholders actually win based on economics because they get to enjoy the tightening of the discount - and tactical investors will agree - however it is unlikely that the majority of current holders actually want to leave the fund.

Retail investors often react badly to corporate actions requiring their vote - a Google search for "Amundi Pioneer" reveals that the second most common question people ask is "Why is Amundi contacting me".

Many investors view funds as a lockbox which they can set and forget, however, that’s not how companies work and, let’s not forget, that a CEF is a company - a registered investment company but still a company - which means that to make certain mundane decisions it needs to go back to shareholders and request permission and if investors don’t care to pay attention then the company can’t go about its normal business.

A liquidation of these funds is an acknowledgment that CEF investors mostly don’t care a whole lot to pay attention and this is a problem for managers. If there is upside, it confirms once again that the CEF market is not very efficient - how can it be when most people aren’t paying attention?

At this point, the funds are trading at discounts of 5-6% which is attractive as a tactical long or as rotations for sector positions that investors already hold. The funds will be selling assets over time, further derisking, meaning the alpha signal will increase relative to the NAV noise.

Stance and Takeaways

This week we further trimmed our CLO Equity CEF allocation. Specifically, we reduced our CCIF and EARN holdings. Sector NAVs are coming in quite a bit lower in April after a big drop in March as well.

Normally, we would be adding to holdings after a big drop (following our valuation-based countercyclical allocation approach) however prices have not adjusted much lower despite NAV falls. This relative price stability along with sharp NAV drops is resulting in very rich valuations.

The following chart shows current valuations as of the last official NAV. ECC, EIC and PDCC numbers are up-to-date as we have their April NAVs. However, the valuations of the other funds (those marked with a question mark) are significantly overstated as they have not released their March NAVs as of this writing.

OXLC valuation is likely overstated 15-20% (i.e. it looks cheaper than it actually is) in the chart while the others are overstated by 4-7%. All of this means that the average valuation in the sector is around a 5-10% premium, which is, frankly, not cheap.

This pattern of rising valuations in a period of lower NAVs tends to happen with CLO Equity CEFs which is the opposite of what the rest of the CEF space does where valuations tend to cheapen (i.e. discounts widen or premiums turn into discounts) when NAVs fall. This makes it difficult to add to CLO Equity CEFs or hold them in weak periods.

data.title: Our Top 10 High-Growth Dividend Stocks -- May 2025
data.description:
Note: Please note that the stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

What a difference a month can make! As we have seen from early April to May, the market sentiment has changed 180 degrees. Not only has the market bounced back from a brief flirtation with the bear market, but it has recovered most of its losses. The market started its recovery after most of the reciprocal tariffs were paused for 90 days. More recently, there was an announcement of a trade pact with the U.K., and some more are expected to be finalized in the coming month. The biggest bump came early this week with the likely compromise on the tariff situation between the U.S. and China. Even though it is only a pause for 90 days, the market has a high confidence that something will get worked out between the two countries, as the previous tariffs on both sides were as good as a trade 'blockade' and were unsustainable. To be sure, there is still a base tariff of 10% on almost every country and 30% tariffs on China, but it is still a big climbdown from 50% or more of reciprocal tariffs on almost every country. There is no doubt that the Trump administration likes to start with the most extreme scenario, but it is flexible enough to compromise in the face of adverse outcomes. The market is showing renewed optimism and a level of confidence in a 'Trump put'. In other words, it has a reasonable level of confidence that President Trump (in his desire to balance the trade and bring manufacturing back home) would still be sensitive enough to the market and not cause permanent damage to the market or the economy.

The Fed is still holding rates steady between 4.25% and 4.50%, at least until June. The probability of a rate cut in June has gone down drastically in recent weeks. The relatively high interest rates are a problem for the economy, especially housing. If the Fed reduces the rates in its June meeting, it will be a positive surprise, and we think that will provide a big boost to the market as well as to strapped-up consumers.

You can see the odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

We can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. However, we intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-year dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 10-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score".

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350–400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: APH, CI, DKS, MS, and RGLD (5 duplicates)

Appeared three times: AVGO, HWM, (4 duplicates).

We are now left with 41 (50 - 9) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There are none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes OVV, SF, DKS, CDW, MOS, ZION, and CTRA (leaving us 34 names).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. This removes QSR, leaving us with 33 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. This removes LLY, leaving us with 32 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major: We keep BCS and BK, but remove BBVA.

Computer, Electronics, and Business Software: We will keep APH and MSFT but remove MSCI, INTU, and ADP.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep AVGO.

Finance – Inv. Banking and others: We will keep MS and HLNE but remove V, BRO, and DFS.

Industrial- Const. & Engg.: We keep HWM and CTAS, but remove GWW.

Gold: We keep RGLD.

Utility: We keep NRG.

REITs: We keep SBAC and AMH but remove EQIX.

We also removed some of the stocks with the lowest relative strength in the list. We also remove MLI, IMO, and BMI.

Please note that we are removing DFS (Discover) because of the pending merger with Capital One (COF). At this stage, keeping DFS will be the same as buying COF; otherwise, we had DFS last month, and it did very well.

We are now left with 20 names.

In all, we removed eleven names, so we are finally left with 20 (32-12) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector):

(HEI), (CTVA), (FIX), (BCS), (BK), (MSFT), (STN), (PWR), (APH), (MS), (HLNE), (HWM), (CI), (RGLD), (MCK), (SBAC), (AMH), (AVGO), (CTAS), and (NRG).

Table 2: Top 20 High Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come up with a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is not a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (May 2025):

(CTVA), (BCS), (MSFT), (STN), (APH), (HWM), (RGLD), (MCK), (AVGO), and (NRG).

Previous Month Selections:

(SSNC), (DFS), (GL), (MA), (HWM), (BRO), (RGLD), (MLI), (CTAS), and (NRG).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. However, this month, a record-breaking 70% of stocks (7 out of 10) from the previous month will no longer be on the new list.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a "hold" (if not a "buy"). However, for new selections, we take into account many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Past Performance

The past month has proved to be a banner month for the picks in spite of a very high volatility. To be sure, the S&P500 also gained nearly 8% during this period. Our portfolio gained roughly 12.5% this past month.

Back testing, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund (VIG). We are presenting the back testing from Jan. 2010 until Apr. 2025. One of our selections, Corteva (CTVA), did not have a history prior to 2020, so for the earlier period, we have only included the rest of the nine stocks. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 15 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

Since the inception of this series, we have provided the progress and performance of the series. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), and also with the S&P500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of Jan. 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio. As expected, the performance this month is muted but not negative, even though both VIG and S&P500 had significant negative returns.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

Overall, we had a highly positive performance from last month's portfolio and ended with a return of 12.4%, while the benchmarks VIG and SPY both had a positive month, especially a nearly 8% gain for the S&P500. The star performers for this month were NRG Energy (NRG), Discover (DFS), and Howmet Aerospace (HWM). However, two stocks, Brown & Brown (BRO) and Royal Gold (RGLD), had significant negative performances. For the next month, we are dropping DFS in spite of very positive momentum due to the pending merger with Capital One. We will also be dropping BRO, but will still retain RGLD.

This month, we are retaining three stocks from the previous month. Seven of the stocks from last month are being replaced, which is unusually high. The new entrants are Corteva (CVTA), Barclays (BCS), Microsoft (MSFT), Stantec (STN), Amphenol (APH), and Broadcom (AVGO).

Please note that our list changes from month to month. These are not buy recommendations as such, but a short list of outstanding stocks to research further.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, especially at the present time. We recommend due diligence.

data.title: Umicore: Searching For The Elusive Bottom
data.description:
Umicore S.A. (OTCPK:UMICF) (OTCPK:UMICY) is a materials technology and recycling company, based in Belgium. The company traces its roots back 200 years, when it started life as a smelting company, quickly diversifying into mining. At the beginning of the century, Umicore made a strategic pivot to align with the megatrends of electrification, battery technology, and the circular economy. Their major business today is as a manufacturer and recycler of catalysts and battery materials for use in the automotive sector.

It is organized today into four major business groups. Batteries, Catalysis, Specialty Materials, and Recycling.

In a world that is moving rapidly towards emissions control and electrification, these are all strategic growth opportunities.

However, significant investment is required to build capacity, and demand has been inconsistent. Nevertheless, in the long run, demand growth for Umicore's products remains inevitable. EV adoption and other battery-powered demand are expected to rise exponentially, and tailwinds for catalytics and cobalt demand are also projected.

I last covered Umicore back in December in an article into introduces the company in detail and goes through each of its segments in some detail. I also provide mid and long-term demand projections. I’d encourage readers new to the company to check out that original piece. I speculated that Umicore might have hit a low point in the company's earnings, and that the share price might have bottomed.

Since then, among broader market turmoil, the share price has continued to decline, although there have been some forays into positive territory along the way.

Since then, Umicore provided FY 2024 earnings, held a capital markets day, and given a Q1 sales update. Let's check in and see how things look today.

Firstly, to put the results in perspective with the company valuation, just a reminder of the significant drop in share price since FY 2023. Umicore shares have dropped from over $22 to $9, and the market cap from over $5.5bn to $2.25bn.

This is the result of significant demand headwinds in each of their operating segments, which have flowed through to the top and bottom line.

Umicore wrote off EUR 1.6bn following a pivot on battery capacity production mid-2024.

FY 2024 Results

As might be expected, the numbers are not pretty year-on-year. Revenues are down around 11% year-on-year, with negative prints in all four segments.

Adjusted EBITDA is down more like 20%.

Management pulled out all the stops to reduce costs, and managed to save EUR 100m vs. the EUR 70m target.

The Net result is a loss of close to EUR 1.5bn, mainly driven by the EUR 1.6bn write-down. Note, this is a non-cash item.

On a positive note, Umicore is throwing off a good deal of cash, with free cash flow actually up 15% year-on-year to EUR 384m. This was helped by a significant reduction in CAPEX as the company adjusted to a slower demand ramp-up.

The company is in decent financial shape, with a leverage ratio of 1.87 x EBITDA, having paid down EUR 159m during 2024.

Capital Markets Day

During the capital markets day, Umicore gave updates on the strategic review that was commissioned by the new CEO, and set targets for the company over the coming three years.

These are for EBITDA to exceed EUR 1bn by 2025, with an upper boundary for both of EUR 1.2bn, cash flow generation of EUR 1-1.2bn over the period, and a ROCE of 15%. This implies a 30-55% increase in EBITDA over three years.

The three foundation businesses are expected to deliver this, despite a continued drag from the battery metals business, which is expected to soak up EUR 600m of cash over the period. A BIG investment into the future of this segment.

The battery metals segment is expected to turn cash flow positive in 2027 after absorbing EUR 370m of CAPEX in production capacity and a EUR 500m equity investment into J.V. IONWAY with PowerCo.

In the shorter term, the overall outlook given is rather stable. Positive performance from Catalysis and, to a lesser extent, Specialty, offset by a slight deterioration of recycling, resulting in the roll off of some precious metal price hedges.

Given the strong performance of precious metals in the period since the capital markets day in March, this outlook will improve, as confirmed by Umicore in its Q1 sales update. The update confirmed 2025 guidance.

Valuation

Umicore trades at close to book value, which is well below its long-term valuation, which had a floor around 2x book for years.

Similarly, at 9x trailing earnings, with an improving earnings outlook, this also screams value.

As does 7.3x trailing FCF.

Peers in the technology materials segment, catalytics and specialty minerals, include Johnson Matthey (OTCPK:JMPLY), Solvay (OTCPK:SVYSF), and Arkema (OTCPK:ARKAY). This seems to trade in a long-term price to FCF range of 10x to 15x.

The lower end of this range implies a 40% upside target for Umicore at current levels of FCF generation.

Conclusion

  • Umicore operates in segments with long-term demand drivers.
  • Cyclical trends have put pressure on its four diverse segments.
  • Most pain has been in the battery metals segment, but here also lies the most long-term opportunity.
  • Core segments are returning to positive momentum.
  • Batteries will be net negative until 2027.
  • Positive FCF and manageable leverage will allow Umicore to invest in batteries.
  • Given the big write-off and strategic pivot, investors are wary.
  • The stock is cheap and seems close to bottoming.
  • The rebound is likely to take a few quarters to materialize.
  • I rate Umicore as a long-term buy, but suggest a cautious approach to establish a position while confirmation of a bottom forms.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: In Search Of A Tailwind, Sailing The Doldrums With Madrigal Pharmaceuticals
data.description:
Madrigal Pharmaceuticals (MDGL) only launched Rezdiffra (resmetirom) for nonalcoholic steatohepatitis [NASH] in April 2024, but the drug has already broken through the $100M per quarter mark, with net sales of $137.3M in Q1 '25 alone. When I covered MDGL in January, I noted the stronger than expected launch and ample room for further growth, rating MDGL a buy. Still, the stock has traded in a channel for about six months now, and currently sits near the bottom of that channel, leading to the question if anything can help the name break out one way or another.

Q1 '25 earnings

Q1 '25 earnings came with a bigger revenue surprise than was seen in Q4 '24, with total revenues of $137.3M (a 20% beat on estimates) coming from entirely from sales of Rezdiffra. The growth of Rezdiffra continues to surprise then, but the news hasn't been matched by a rally to new highs.

Other financials include cost of sales of $4.5M for Q1 '25, R&D expenses of $44.2M for the quarter, and SG&A of $167.9M for the quarter. Net loss was $73.2M in Q1 '25, with net cash used in operating activities of $88.9M for the quarter. MDGL isn't profitable then, but since there is the potential for other drugs to be approved for NASH, leaning into the first mover advantage by marketing the drug heavily now, even if it means reporting losses each quarter for a while, makes sense.

MDGL finished Q1 '25 with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $843.1M, the company also had a further $5M in restricted cash. MDGL does have $118M of loan payable, net of discount, on the balance sheet, but still with net cash of $730M, and using under $90M per quarter, MDGL is well funded to continue on spending on the launch of Rezdiffra as it has been.

MDGL had 22,203,282 shares of its common stock outstanding as of April 28, 2025. MDGL has a market cap of $6.48B ($291.82).

Potential catalysts ahead

Expansion ex-US

With regards to going beyond the US, MDGL has confirmed that a decision from the European Medicines Agency (EMA) should come in mid-2025, which would pave the way for a launch in Europe within 2025. When I first covered MDGL in April 2024, and the company was launching Rezdiffra in the US, I noted the only competition in NASH was off-label, such as from Novo Nordisk's (NVO) semaglutide and Eli Lilly & Co's (LLY) tirzepatide. Further, I noted the shortage of those incretins could aid Rezdiffra's initial launch. With a potential EU launch, should the company be successful on that front, there is also no approved competition in NASH, so again it is down to off-label options to slow any launch of Rezdiffra.

On the other hand, drugs like Ozempic aren't seeing the shortage they once were. For example, Diabetes UK noted the end of the Ozempic shortage in early 2025, and the US FDA noted the end of the shortage in February 2025 too. A shortage of incretins in the EU aiding a Rezdiffra launch seems unlikely then, but many patients end up on a combination of Rezdiffra and GLP-1 receptor agonist drugs, with Earnest Analytics noting in September 2024 that 35% of patients taking Rezdiffra had recent GLP-1 receptor agonist experience. That 35% number fits pretty well with numbers from MDGL, of about 25% of Rezdiffra patients being on a GLP-1 receptor agonist.

So, as we reported previously, 25% of patients are on a GLP-1 that are on Rezdiffra. That's our estimate right now. And it jumps to about 50% that have been exposed previously. So, GLP-1s, we have to remember, they're not new. They've been here for over a decade. Despite them being here for over a decade, there's still this MASH challenge that exists. People are still diagnosing and seeing MASH and patients are progressing.

Bill Sibold, CEO of MDGL, Q1 '25 earnings call.

Further clinical data

To expand the reach of Rezdiffra, MDGL's MAESTRO-NASH OUTCOMES trial is expected to produce results in 2027. The current approval of Rezdiffra covers NASH patients with "moderate to advanced liver fibrosis (consistent with stages F2 to F3 fibrosis)." By comparison, MAESTRO-NASH OUTCOMES enrolls patients with compensated MASH cirrhosis (F4c), an indication which, if MDGL is successful, could add billions to the market potential of Rezdiffra.

A 2027 readout isn't a near-term event. Still, a potential surrogate for an improvement in outcomes is the data presented recently from the open-label extension of MAESTRO-NAFLD-1. That data showed a reduction in liver stiffness in F4c patients following resmetirom treatment, and since an elevation in liver stiffness is an important predictor of a worse clinical outcome in liver disease, those results might bode well for the chances of success in MAESTRO-NASH OUTCOMES.

Competitor developments

The recent publication of data from ESSENCE, a trial of semaglutide in MASH, and the news that NVO has filed for approval of semaglutide in MASH in the EU and US, are a reminder that competition could end up being fierce in NASH/MASH. Indeed, NVO noted on April 30 that its application for US approval of semaglutide for MASH has been accepted for priority review. Updates on that review, such as approval, would likely represent bad news for MDGL. Given NVO just spoke of priority review (six months) at the end of April, I would suggest approval of semaglutide for MASH might not come until Q4 '25. I still believe Rezdiffra compares favorably to semaglutide in terms of the ability of patients to stay on the drug, whereas the recent ESSENCE data come from 2.4 mg semaglutide, and discontinuations could be a real-world issue with semaglutide.

Nonetheless, I believe the market is concerned about MDGL's competition, not just from approved agents but other agents in the pipeline. For example, one upcoming catalyst would be data from IMPACT, a phase 2b trial of Altimmune's (ALT) pemvidutide in F2/F3 MASH patients. That trial overenrolled (212 vs the expected 190 patients) is randomized and placebo-controlled, and has the potential to show a benefit on NASH resolution, reduction in fibrosis and weight loss at just 24 weeks. ALT has recently confirmed with Q1 '25 earnings that top-line data is expected in Q2 '25.

Business development

MDGL has spoken openly, including on the recent Q1 '25 conference call, about its desire to have additional products beyond Rezdiffra, but still within MASH. As such, news that MDGL has acquired another asset could come at any time. MDGL does speak of financial discipline, so there are certain assets out there that might be beyond the company's reach right now, and if those are what the company wants, then it may wait for the launch of Rezdiffra to continue to scale up before doing a deal.

The focus will be in MASH. And we're looking at next mechanisms of action that are compelling or products that could be combined with Rezdiffra to make Rezdiffra and the new entity that much better. So that allows us to either extend efficacy, work in new different -- pardon me -- different segments of the population, et cetera. So, we're looking across the entire pipeline, early stage to late stage. We're going to base it on mechanisms that we're interested in, mechanisms that we think could be helpful for patients. And we will then be very diligent in our -- in the way that we actually go about doing our deals. We're not going to do a bet the company strategy on any of this. We don't have to. So, we'll maintain that financial discipline.

Bill Sibold, CEO of MDGL, Q1 '25 earnings call.

It seems more likely if there is development on this front in the near-term, that an earlier stage asset might be the more likely acquisition.

Conclusions

The Rezdiffra launch didn't just surprise on net sales in Q1 '25, with 33% growth vs. Q4 '24, the number of patients on therapy grew 44%, from 11,800 at the end of Q4 '24 to 17,000 at the end of Q1 '25. As such, underlying demand is growing at an even greater rate, than might be reflected in net sales growth, even though that sales growth was strong enough to drive a 20% earnings surprise on Q1 '25's revenues. Further, the company believes only 5% of the applicable market (diagnosed F2-F3 NASH patients) are on Rezdiffra, and so I'm not expecting the launch to hit a ceiling any time soon, even as the evidence for competing agents like semaglutide continues to build.

I could make the bull case that with a strong Q2 '25 likely incoming, and EMA approval seeming likely, the market might be forced to acknowledge again the strength of this launch, and the peak revenues that may lie ahead, come Q2 '25 earnings. Additionally, with the stock on the lower end of its recent range, but without a net negative change in the position of the company, at least in my opinion, I think there is room for upside in the stock. As such, I rate MDGL a buy currently.

The risks of a long in MDGL are several-fold, a few of which I'll consider here. Firstly, MDGL could report delays in approval in Europe or even outright rejection of Rezdiffra.

Secondly, MDGL might make an acquisition the market is unimpressed by, and given the cash used on the acquisition, the stock could fall.

Lastly, if Q2 '25 sales show growth over Q1 '25, but not to the extent the market was hoping for, the stock could fall. If Q2 '25 sales are lower than Q1 '25 sales of Rezdiffra, then the fall would likely be particularly pronounced.

data.title: Ride High But Stay Dry: Why I'm Accumulating Cash As The Market Rebounds
data.description:
Déjà vu, anyone?

This market rally is starting to feel a lot like the V-shaped stock market recovery in the early months of COVID-19.

I was surprised how fast the market recovered back in April-May 2020, and I'm surprised at how fast it has recovered in April-May 2025 as well.

When the market's movements don't make sense, I find that it's best to sit back, observe, and do nothing until I have a better grasp of the situation. That goes for individual stocks, too.

To modify a quote from Abraham Lincoln:

It's better to make no portfolio changes and be thought a fool than to make too many changes and remove all doubt.

Below, I'll discuss why I have turned to accumulating cash rather than investing the incremental dollar into stocks. Given the fact that my favorite cash-like vehicle still yields 4.7%, I find little downside in choosing to allocate new capital this way.

But before getting to that subject, there are a few other points to touch on in this week's article:

  • More on the tug-of-war between recession and inflation in the 10-year, and why it's tilted in a certain direction today.
  • Why is China better prepared to outlast the US in this trade conflict than the other way around.
  • The most recent version of the Congressional budget reconciliation package shows that huge deficit spending is not going to end anytime soon.
  • A deep dive into life science real estate and what to do with the beaten-down Alexandria stock.
  • How sexy, high-beta growth has mounted a surprising comeback against defensive dividend stocks.

Onward.

The 10-Year Treasury Says No Recession But Higher Inflation

As I discussed in last week's article, the 10-year Treasury yield is a good indicator of whether recession or inflation is the greater risk at the present moment.

When recession becomes more likely, the disinflationary effects of economic weakness outweigh any inflation impact of tariffs, and the 10-year rate falls. A 145% tariff rate is more of an embargo that cuts off all trade rather than a tax that flows through into price increases.

When recession becomes less likely in the face of lower (but still elevated) tariffs, inflation becomes more likely because businesses will import but seek to pass through tariff costs to the end consumer. This inevitable (albeit temporary) bump in inflation pushes up the 10-year rate.

You can see this dynamic at play in the dance of the 10-year yield:

In the days after the April 2nd “Liberation Day” tariffs announcement, the market immediately priced in a massive drop in international trade as well as a global recession.

Then the “sell America” trade took off, pushing up US interest rates at the same time as stock prices fell.

In late April, the market again began pricing in recession as the White House wouldn't budge on Chinese import tariffs.

Finally, after the 90-day reduction in tariffs to 30%, recession odds again declined, but inflation expectations increased. A 30% tariff rate is low enough not to be prohibitive, but also high enough to necessitate price hikes.

A 40% chance of recession still isn't comforting, but the sheer relief from the end of the China embargo was enough to bring back some optimism.

With tariffs at 145%, there is no solution for many American businesses that rely on Chinese suppliers. At 30%, most of these businesses can probably work something out, whether that means absorbing the cost themselves, negotiating with suppliers, or passing the cost along to customers in the form of price hikes.

As the Wall Street Journal editorial board recently put it:

The 30% tariff is still exceptionally high for a major trading partner, but the 90-day rollback spares both sides from what looked like an impending economic crackup. U.S. consumers were facing widespread shortages, while China feared growing unemployment.

There are three important caveats to the optimism about this bilateral de-escalation in tariff rates.

First, even with such a massive drop from 145% to 30%, tariffs on imports from China are still very high in an absolute sense. They could only be considered “low” relative to the absurdly high 145% peak. Having to pay a 30% tax on the value of all imports from China will still result in price hikes, margin compression, and probably some business failures/bankruptcies.

Second, forgive me for sounding harsh, but what exactly was gained from the “leverage” of 145% tariffs? What America-friendly concession was granted by the Chinese government in exchange for this de-escalation? How are American businesses better off today than they were before “Liberation Day”?

Both the big businesses in the S&P 500 (SPY) and the smaller publicly traded businesses in the Russell 2000 (IWM) are up since the day before the Rose Garden announcement:

Again, how are American businesses, big or small, better off today than before April 2nd?

To quote the WSJ editorial board again:

As with last week’s modest British agreement, the China deal is more surrender than the Trump victory. Apart from the tariff rollback, neither side announced any broader concessions on the substantive trade issues that weigh on the U.S.-China relationship. Those include China’s barriers to American firms, especially in services such as digital and financial, and its chronic intellectual property theft.

What exactly, then, is the market so happy about?

I'm left scratching my head.

The third caveat to the recent market optimism is that 30% tariffs on America's 3rd largest trading partner will simultaneously increase inflation while eating into corporate margins. Both are bad for the economy and, one would think, the stock market.

While most business economists don't expect inflation to show up in the data until late May or early June (if not later), the real-time consumer inflation monitor Truflation is showing what appears to be a small resurgence in inflation in mid-May:

Whether that trend continues is something to watch.

But at this point, it is a foregone conclusion that there will be some inflationary impact on the 10% across-the-board (except for carve-outs like iPhones) tariffs, 30% Chinese tariffs, and 25% tariffs on steel, aluminum, and autos.

The Yale Budget Lab estimates that, inclusive of substitutionary tariff avoidance, the new average effective tariff rate stands at 14%. That is still higher than any level since the 1930s.

Walmart (WMT) CFO John David Rainey has stated that 30% tariffs are simply too much for foreign suppliers and American businesses to absorb:

We’re wired for everyday low prices, but the magnitude of these increases is more than any retailer can absorb. It’s more than any supplier can absorb. And so I’m concerned that consumers are going to start seeing higher prices. You’ll begin to see that, likely towards the tail end of this month, and then certainly much more in June.

I think WMT's stock price has held up well, even at a nosebleed 37x P/E ratio, because higher prices combined with consumer trepidation should cause a trade-down effect. How do you save money on essential items? By shopping at the lowest-cost provider. That is very often WMT.

There's a wide variety of consumer goods for which the US is heavily reliant on Chinese suppliers, including children's toys, simple electronics, toiletries, and fireworks.

(It's ironic that the very fireworks used to celebrate American independence are overwhelmingly made in China, were invented by the Chinese, and probably wouldn't be economical to produce in the US.)

Another reason why inflation is inevitable is the closing of the “de minimis” loophole that allowed Chinese retailers like Temu and Shein to ship under-$800 orders directly to American consumers duty-free. Last year, de minimis shipments totaled $1.36 billion.

Now, there's potentially a silver lining here. If consumers aren't buying from Temu and Shein, perhaps they'll buy more from Walmart, Amazon (AMZN), and discount retailers like TJ Maxx (TJX) and Ross (ROST).

Surely, some of this consumer spending on ultra-cheap items simply won't happen anymore, but to the degree that American retailers step in to substitute for de minimis shipments, they could be net beneficiaries.

Consumers, however, are unmitigatedly worse off, as the result in any case is higher prices paid for more-or-less equivalent items.

Don't Shoot The Messenger, But We Need Them More Than They Need Us

It bears mentioning that in spite of this helpful de-escalation, China trade risks remain. In my view, substantial concessions are unlikely to be forthcoming from the Chinese government anytime soon.

One reason for this is that China has steadily reduced its export reliance on the US over time:

Moreover, total Chinese exports have not fallen in the wake of the most recent US-China trade spat. CNBC reported that in April, Chinese exports to the US dropped 21% YoY, but total Chinese exports increased 8.1% YoY. This was due largely to a surge in Chinese exports to Southeast Asian countries and, to a lesser extent, growth in exports to Europe.

The US suffers worse from a two-way embargo than China does.

That leads to the second reason why substantial concessions from China are unlikely to be forthcoming anytime soon, and that is the fact that the US is going it alone in its quest to reform Chinese trade practices.

If the US had a tighter trade alliance with other Asian countries (such as what would've been with the Trans Pacific Partnership), we could collectively pressure China with far greater leverage than the US has alone. The same is true with our European allies, who are importing more from China to offset the Chinese loss of American buyers.

Finally, a third reason why China looks poised to outlast the US in these trade tensions is its political system. President Xi Jinping does not face elections every two years like the American government does, and the Chinese people are culturally more geared toward long-term thinking than the American people are.

If the market is pricing in significant concessions from China, I think it's pricing in too much.

Another Reason Why The 10-Year Is Rising: Endless Deficit Spending

The non-partisan Committee For A Responsible Federal Budget scored the most recent version of the Congressional budget bill, and the results aren't pretty.

For all the talk of DOGE, Medicaid cuts, and renewable energy tax credit rollbacks, Congress still looks likely to pass a reconciliation package that adds trillions of dollars to the fiscal deficit over the next decade:

Over a 10-year period, the most recent version of the budget package is estimated to add ~$3.3 trillion to ~$5.2 trillion to the national debt. This includes both tax cuts and spending increases for the military and immigration enforcement.

There will be a day of reckoning for this persistent fiscal excess, but I don't think we're there yet.

What Do You Do With A Problem Like Alexandria?

Ay yi yi.

I've spent the last few years accumulating a large position in Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) at an average cost basis of around $118. Or, at least, it was a large position at one point. I'm now down some 40% from my cost basis.

Since the peak in late 2021/early 2022, ARE is down by 2/3rds, while the only other REIT with material life science exposure – Healthpeak Properties (DOC) – is down over 50%.

(I'm down “only” 14% on my DOC position, which is a more recent addition to my portfolio.)

What is going on here? Are these REITs' dividends safe? Is there a hope for a return to growth in the future? Or are these life science buildings doomed to become high-vacancy money pits for the foreseeable future?

In the past, my investment thesis has been that the sheer quality of ARE's portfolio and balance sheet will sustain it through whatever difficult period that awaits it.

But there are a number of headwinds to life science CRE that must be acknowledged:

  • Oversupply of life science properties is present in every major R&D hub in the US.
  • The vacancy rate for US life science reached 20% by the end of 2024, which is about the same rate as for the traditional office sector.
  • Major markets like Boston, San Diego, and New York City are seeing double-digit declines in asking rent rates. (Boston is ARE's largest market.)
  • Biotech companies are making increasing use of AI in drug discovery, which may end up reducing space needs.
  • Cuts to NIH research funding incrementally reduce demand for life science space.
  • Layoffs at the FDA have lengthened the drug approval process.
  • A recent poll found that 75% of US scientists are considering leaving the US to work in another country like Canada or Europe, which could lead to a brain drain and reduced demand for life science space.
  • Drug price controls would reduce incentives to invest in R&D. Per the Tax Foundation: "Drug pricing legislation would lower prices for some existing drugs, which would likely cause a reduction in R&D investment because the expected returns to new drug development would fall. While lower prices would lead to increased use of existing drugs, less R&D would lead to fewer new drugs."

There are also a few important tailwinds to life science real estate to consider:

  • Aging demographics render an upward trajectory of demand for prescription drugs as far as the eye can see into the future.
  • Leasing activity remained steady in 2024 and Q1 2025, even though the sheer level of supply deliveries more than offset this demand.
  • The construction pipeline for new life science buildings has shrunk considerably.

From a peak of 17% of total inventory, the life science construction pipeline has fallen to 7%, which is about the same level as it was in 2021, before the post-COVID surge in life science development projects got underway.

At this point, ARE's AFFO payout ratio hovers around 70-75%, which is comfortable, but cash flow is expected to be flattish for at least the next two years.

If this forecast proves accurate, and if cash flow actually does return to steady growth in 2027, then ARE looks like a great buying opportunity today.

At a dividend yield of 7.4% and an FFO multiple of 7.7x, ARE is very cheap even in a permanently low-growth future.

But is it that easy? The market is pricing in a lot of pessimism—more than the analysts. The market is basically saying that there's no visible light at the end of the tunnel for life science real estate, and that ARE's high-quality portfolio won't save it from the pain.

"The Year of the Dividend" Didn't Last Long

In previous articles such as this one, I highlighted how dividend ETFs such as the iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV), Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), and Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM) were outperforming the broad indices like the SPY, Nasdaq index (QQQ), and even the vaunted Magnificent 7 (MAGS) year-to-date.

Emphasis on the word "were."

After the China tariffs pause rally over the last week, the picture is far more muddled.

MAGS is now performing better than SCHD year-to-date.

QQQ is performing better than VYM.

SCHD's shift into oil producers after its March reconstitution continues to look untimely.

Defensive value stocks' brief time in the sun seems to have ended. The reign of mega-cap growth has resumed.

And as another sign of the market's rapid shift back to risk-on mode, Bitcoin has mounted a resurgence back near its all-time high.

While gold acts as a defensive hedge against perceived fiscal excesses and economic risks, Bitcoin is basically a proxy for high-beta stocks.

Defensive, low-beta dividend growers seem to have gone out of fashion again, while high-beta growth stocks have gone back to being all the rage.

Why I'm Accumulating Cash Today

First, I must clarify my meaning because lots of readers mistook me in last week's article (perhaps due to a fault in my own communication). Last week, I made the case for raising cash right now. By that, I did not mean to sell stocks. I'm not selling anything right now.

Instead, what I meant was to gradually accumulate cash by halting dividend reinvestment and putting new, investable savings into a cash-like vehicle.

I should further clarify that 99.9% of my portfolio is invested in various non-cash securities, mostly dividend stocks. My current cash position is tiny.

If you already have 5-10% or more of your portfolio in cash, you can just ignore what I'm choosing to do right now.

But I want to set a long-term goal to eventually raise my cash position to 3-5% of my total portfolio value during periods in which the S&P 500 is less than 5% off its all-time high. The purpose of this cash cushion is to act as dry powder for corrections.

And I want to increase my cash position during times like now, when (1) conspicuously good buying opportunities are elusive to me, and (2) the yield on cash-like vehicles is higher than the yield of most dividend stocks of interest to me.

To quote myself (one of my favorite people to quote) from a late September 2024 article called "Raise Cash And Sell Trash":

[W]hat is a strident DGIer [dividend growth investor] to do when attractive buying opportunities are scarce or nonexistent? What is a DGIer to do when the market looks richly valued and the economy seems dangerously close to slipping into recession?

My answer to that question is to accumulate cash.

This isn't market-timing, in my view, because I'm not selling anything. Rather, I'm incrementally allocating investable dollars into cash instead of DGI stocks. It's a strategic decision for my own portfolio, not a macro call that the stock market is about to crash. It's a fool's errand to make such calls.

My preferred cash-like vehicle is the Vanguard Ultra-Short Term Bond ETF (VUSB), which mostly holds investment-grade corporate bonds with an average maturity of 1.4 years. The ETF yields 4.7% and sports an expense ratio of 0.1%.

I often get asked why I don't instead choose an AAA collateralized loan obligation ETF like the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) or the iShares AAA CLO Active ETF (CLOA), which both sport expense ratios of 0.2% and yield about 5.5% right now.

Well, CLOs (even AAA-rated ones) are somewhat riskier than bonds, which is why these two ETFs exhibit more downside during market panics:

This higher volatility dampens CLO ETFs' appeal as a dry powder because I don't want my cash-like vehicle to be down (even by 2%) at exactly the time I want to use it to buy stocks.

The 80 basis points of additional yield is appealing, of course, but it may not be worth the additional volatility.

On the other hand, you've got even less volatility with ultra-short-term Treasury ETFs like iShares 0-3 Month Treasury ETF (SGOV), the Global X 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (CLIP), and the Vanguard Ultra-Short Treasury ETF (VGUS), but these all have yields about 50 basis points lower than VUSB.

And then there are the sexy, actively managed ultra-short-term bond ETFs like PIMCO Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT), the iShares Short Duration Bond Active ETF (NEAR), and the JP Morgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST), but these all come with somewhat higher expense ratios of 0.18% to 0.35% while offering no premium in yield.

The closest thing to VUSB would be the iShares Ultra-Short Duration Bond Active ETF (ICSH), which has an expense ratio of 0.08% and yields 4.6%. This is basically BlackRock's (BLK) version of VUSB. (Or, rather, VUSB is Vanguard's version of ICSH, since the latter has been around far longer.)

Since I use Vanguard as my investing platform, I go with VUSB. But there's little reason to choose it over ICSH as far as I can tell.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Consumer Spending Strong - Intermodal Volume Growth Decelerates
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus, I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

April data included a small increase in consumer prices, but a significant decline in producer prices. Nominal increase in retail sales rose slightly, but declined in real terms. Industrial production was unchanged, but manufacturing production declined. Housing starts increased slightly, but both total and single family permits declined significantly. Consumer sentiment as to both the present and future conditions as initially measured by the U. Of Michigan declined further to near 20-year lows.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 6.28%, up +0.02% w/w (1-yr range: 5.33-6.38)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.44%, up +0.05% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.84%, down -0.03% w/w (1.36-2.02).

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.45%, up +0.06% w/w (-1.07 - +0.53)
  • 10 year minus 3 month: +0.09%, up +0.01% w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.34%, up +0.10% w/w.

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.92%, up +0.03% w/w (6.11-7.52).

Treasury and corporate bond yields are well below their 12-month highs of several months ago, as are mortgage rates. Nevertheless they remain a negative. The spreads between Treasuries and investment grade and high-yield bonds, which blew out in early April, have returned to their previous range.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps up +2% to 154 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. up +1 to 145 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +18% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +10% [NSA]
  • Refi apps down -0.4% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +44% [SA].

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted.

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Down less than -0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.2% YoY.

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now higher YoY, but are close to their longer-term bottoms, so they score neutral.

In 2023, real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12-month-high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. March data was just released three weeks ago:

  • M1 m/m up +0.2%, YoY Real M1 up +0.8% (3 year high) (worst: -1.4% 8/23)
  • M2 m/m up +0.4%, YoY Real M2 up +1.7% (3 year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it is either during the latter part of a recession, or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (Q1 90% actual + 10% estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet) at p. 33

  • Q1 up +0.12 to 64.01, down -2.7% q/q.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. That briefly happened early this quarter, which means this metric turned negative. But as earnings have come in, it has improved sharply from its original -8% range all the way to neutral.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index down -0.07 (looser) to -0.51 (-0.42 - -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) down -0.06 (looser) to -0.57(-0.50 - -0.69)
  • Leverage subindex down -0.16 (looser) to -0.37 (+0.13 - -0.76).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. Recently, this index went below -0.60, warranting a rating change to positive, but they have since retreated back into the neutral range. Despite its recent big upward move, the leverage subindex was still positive for the economy; and it has also improved in the past few weeks.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): up +34 to 421, up +31 m/m (209 5/24/24 - 421 5/16/25) Components: Yield curve 50 Phillips curve 371.
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: down -0.1326 to -0.4793 (+0.0944 4/18/25 - -.9762 1/31/25).
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: up +10.3 to +49.0 as of 5/1/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead).

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices(expecting a reversal) which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several weeks ago.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It has now recovered.

Since last April IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession.

Trade weighted US$

  • Up +0.02 to 122.88 w/w, up +0.4% YoY (last week) (broad) (118.76 - 129.67) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Up +0.71 to 101.08 w/w, down -3.2% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01- 109.66).

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged to 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the last couple of months. The broad US$ retreated under that threshold five weeks ago, and remains neutral, while the major currencies index declined to significantly lower YoY, so a positive.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Down -1.80 to 100.96 (93.30 - 107.71)
  • Down -4.5% YoY.

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx.)

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 142.00, up +.0.35 w/w (130.47 -173.08)
  • Down -12.2% YoY.

Both of these measures improved earlier last year and industrial commodities surged to new 12 month highs in the autumn before backing off.
